nativeRange.habitat: Shallow lagoons and coastal reefs, typically found under ledges or around coral heads on sand or rubble substrate where they excavate extensive tunnel systems.
nativeRange.biotope: Indo-Pacific coastal reef with sand or rubble substrate

decorPreferences: deep sand or fine rubble substrate for tunnelling
extremely stable rockwork resting on the tank bottom (not on sand)
rocks anchored against tank walls to prevent collapse
open swimming space with caves and ledges

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied carnivore diet of frozen mysis, brine shrimp, krill, bloodworms, and finely chopped marine seafood. Has been reported to readily take prepared and frozen foods. Best offered small feedings at least twice daily.

avoidWith: very small fish that could fit in its mouth

summary: The Engineer Goby is an eel-like Indo-Pacific reef fish renowned for excavating elaborate tunnel systems through sand and rubble. Despite its common name, it belongs to its own family and is not a true goby. Juveniles display bold horizontal black-and-white stripes; adults shift to a vertical bar pattern.
careNotes: Despite the common names 'Engineer Goby' and 'Convict Goby', this species is NOT a true goby — it is the sole described member of family Pholidichthyidae, more closely allied to eels and cichlid-line perciforms (placed in order Cichliformes). Juveniles, which start out at around 2.5–7 cm, display striking horizontal black-and-white stripes that closely resemble those of the venomous striped eel catfish (Plotosus lineatus) — a likely defensive mimicry. Adults, reaching roughly 30–34 cm, shift to vertical bars or broken bars. Engineer Gobies are compulsive tunnel and cave builders that will move significant amounts of sand and excavate burrows under and around rockwork. ALL rockwork must rest directly on the tank bottom (on the glass itself) and ideally lean against tank walls — rocks set on top of sand can be undermined and collapse, which has been reported to crush tankmates or crack tanks. A deep sand or fine rubble bed of around 8 cm or more supports their natural digging behaviour. They are generally considered reef-safe in the sense that they do not eat coral tissue, but they will redistribute sand onto coral bases, and adults have been reported to consume very small ornamental shrimp and tiny fish that can fit in the mouth, so they are best added to mixed reef systems with caution. Engineer Gobies are social shoaling fish that tend to do better when kept in pairs or small groups introduced simultaneously; lone specimens are commonly considered more reclusive. They have been reported to live around 7–11 years in well-kept aquariums.
breedingNotes: Engineer Gobies have been reported to spawn in aquaria when kept as a pair or small group, sometimes without any deliberate conditioning. Adults excavate a chamber within their tunnel system and guard the eggs; fry are reported to emerge from the tunnel mouth and can be raised on rotifers and small live foods. Captive breeding remains uncommonly attempted at scale.

Provided minimum tank size (55 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), diet, juvenile-vs-adult colour phase, rockwork stability warning, and 'reef safe with caution' guidance.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Pholidichthyidae, Cichliformes), maximum size (34 cm TL), depth range (3–30 m), temperature (22–28°C), distribution (Western Central Pacific: Philippines to Solomon Islands), and juvenile mimicry of Plotosus lineatus.

additional.name: EpicAquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (up to 34 cm), tank size (30 gal juveniles / 55 gal = ~208 L adults), temperature (22–25°C), salinity (1.020–1.025), lifespan (7–11 years), and compulsive burrowing/tunnel behaviour.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs/groups in established aquaria have been reported to spawn within burrow chambers; raising fry through metamorphosis is challenging due to small mouth size and need for live planktonic foods.
